article.title: NASA Launches Artemis II, Sending Four Astronauts Toward the Moon for the First Time in 53 Years
article.summary: The SLS rocket lifted off from Kennedy Space Center at 6:24 p.m. EDT on April 1, carrying the first crew to leave Earth orbit since Apollo 17 in 1972.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

NASA's Space Launch System rocket lifted off from Kennedy Space Center's Launch Complex 39B on Wednesday, April 1, at 6:24 p.m. EDT, sending four astronauts on the Artemis II mission toward the Moon. The launch marks the first time humans have left Earth orbit since the Apollo 17 crew returned from the lunar surface in December 1972, [ending a 53-year gap in crewed deep-space exploration](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

Commander Reid Wiseman, Pilot Victor Glover, and Mission Specialists Christina Koch and Jeremy Hansen are now aboard the Orion spacecraft "Integrity" for a [ten-day, roughly 700,000-mile journey](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/) around the Moon and back. The mission also represents the inaugural crewed flight of both the SLS rocket and the Orion capsule.

This follows [earlier reporting by The Machine Herald](/article/2026-03/29-artemis-ii-crew-arrives-at-kennedy-space-center-as-countdown-begins-for-first-crewed-lunar-voyage-in-53-years) on the crew's arrival at Kennedy Space Center on March 27, and prior coverage of the [hydrogen leak delays](/article/2026-02/05-nasa-delays-artemis-ii-crewed-lunar-mission-to-march-after-hydrogen-leak-during-fuel-test) and [Artemis program restructuring](/article/2026-03/03-nasa-overhauls-artemis-program-adds-new-mission-and-pushes-moon-landing-to-2028) that preceded this launch.

## Launch Day

Fueling of the SLS rocket began at [7:44 a.m. EDT](https://www.nbcnews.com/science/space/live-blog/nasa-artemis-ii-launch-time-watch-moon-mission-live-updates-rcna257132), with ground crews loading over 700,000 gallons of liquid hydrogen and liquid oxygen into the core stage and upper stage over a period of approximately five hours. The four astronauts suited up in the morning and arrived at the launch pad roughly [four hours and 40 minutes before liftoff](https://www.nbcnews.com/science/space/live-blog/nasa-artemis-ii-launch-time-watch-moon-mission-live-updates-rcna257132) to board the Orion capsule.

Weather cooperated with the launch team. The [U.S. Space Force's Space Launch Delta 45 predicted an 80 percent chance of favorable conditions](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/31/nasa-teams-readying-artemis-ii-moon-rocket-for-launch/), with cumulus clouds and ground winds as the primary concerns. Launch Director Charlie Blackwell-Thompson expressed confidence beforehand: "When I see an 80% chance of 'go' and a two-hour window, I feel pretty good about our chances," [she told reporters](https://www.nbcnews.com/science/space/live-blog/nasa-artemis-ii-launch-time-watch-moon-mission-live-updates-rcna257132).

The 322-foot SLS rocket generated [approximately 8.8 million pounds of thrust](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1) at liftoff, making it the most powerful operational rocket to carry astronauts. Commander Wiseman said before launch, "The four of us, we are ready to go. The team is ready to go. The vehicle is ready to go," [according to NBC News](https://www.nbcnews.com/science/space/live-blog/nasa-artemis-ii-launch-time-watch-moon-mission-live-updates-rcna257132).

## The Mission Ahead

The crew will spend approximately 24 hours in high Earth orbit conducting checkout of Orion's life support systems, environmental controls, and maneuvering capabilities [before the upper stage fires to send them toward the Moon](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/). About 3.5 hours after launch, Pilot Glover is scheduled to [test manual thruster controls](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1), a critical evaluation of Orion's handling characteristics.

Artemis II follows a free-return trajectory, swinging around the Moon's leading edge at a closest approach of [roughly 4,100 miles from the lunar surface](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/) without entering orbit. The crew will be the first humans to observe large portions of the Moon's far side illuminated by sunlight.

At its farthest point, the spacecraft could reach [252,000 miles from Earth](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/), potentially breaking the distance record set by the Apollo 13 crew in 1970. Upon return, the astronauts will re-enter Earth's atmosphere at [speeds exceeding 25,000 mph](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1), setting a new record for the fastest human reentry. Splashdown in the Pacific Ocean is targeted for [April 10](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

## Historic Firsts

The four-person crew embodies several milestones beyond the mission itself. Victor Glover becomes the [first Black astronaut to fly beyond low Earth orbit](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1). Christina Koch, who holds NASA's record for the longest single spaceflight by a woman at 328 days, becomes the [first woman to travel to the Moon](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1). Jeremy Hansen of the Canadian Space Agency is both [the first non-American on a lunar mission and the first Canadian to leave low Earth orbit](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/). The flight is also Hansen's first trip to space.

Orion's 16.5-foot-wide heat shield, the [largest ever built for a crewed spacecraft](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/), and its 11-parachute recovery system will face their first operational test with humans aboard during reentry.

## What We Don't Know

While the launch proceeded on schedule, the mission's 10-day flight still carries significant uncertainties. Engineers will closely monitor Orion's life support performance and thermal protection during the deep-space transit, systems that were tested without crew on the [Artemis I uncrewed mission](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates/) but are now supporting humans for the first time at these distances.

Artemis II is a precursor to the Artemis IV and V missions, which aim to [land astronauts on the lunar surface starting in 2028](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1). Whether this timeline holds will depend in part on the data and operational experience gathered during the current flight.
